prompt: The primary purpose of hemodialysis is to:

options.text: Remove excess fluid and uremic wastes and help correct electrolyte imbalances
Replace the need for any dietary restrictions permanently
Cure chronic kidney disease in one session
Deliver intravenous antibiotics only

explanation: Hemodialysis partially replaces filtration/fluid balance functions of failed kidneys; it is not a cure.

distractorExplanations.c: CKD/ESKD is managed, not cured by a single HD run.
distractorExplanations.d: Antibiotics may be given but are not the primary purpose.

prompt: Diffusion in the dialyzer refers to:

options.text: Fluid removal driven only by hydrostatic pressure
Movement of solutes from higher to lower concentration across a semipermeable membrane
Clotting of the extracorporeal circuit
Air entering the venous chamber

explanation: Diffusion clears small solutes; ultrafiltration removes fluid via pressure gradients.

distractorExplanations.a: That describes ultrafiltration more than diffusion.
distractorExplanations.c: Clotting is a complication, not diffusion.
distractorExplanations.d: Air entry is a safety hazard, not diffusion.

prompt: Ultrafiltration (UF) during hemodialysis primarily removes:

options.text: Only potassium by diffusion without fluid loss
Red blood cells intentionally
Excess fluid from the patient
Dialysate concentrate bottles

explanation: UF volume is prescribed to achieve target post-dialysis weight; excessive UF risks hypotension.

distractorExplanations.b: RBC loss is not the goal.
distractorExplanations.d: Concentrate handling is machine supply, not UF.

prompt: A dialyzer’s semipermeable membrane allows:

options.text: Free mixing of blood and dialysate into one open pool
Bacteria to freely enter the blood path by design
Complete impermeability to all solutes
Passage of water and small/middle molecules while retaining blood cells and most proteins (depending on membrane)

explanation: Membrane selectivity is fundamental; blood and dialysate remain in separate compartments.

distractorExplanations.b: Intact systems aim to prevent contamination.

prompt: Conductivity of dialysate is monitored because it reflects:

options.text: Electrolyte concentration of the dialysate mixture
Patient hemoglobin only
Room humidity exclusively
Needle gauge size

explanation: Out-of-range conductivity can indicate improper concentrate mixing—unsafe for treatment.

distractorExplanations.b: Hgb is a patient lab value.

prompt: Dialysate that is too hot can cause:

options.text: Improved membrane clotting only as a benefit
Hemolysis and patient injury
No physiologic effect
Automatic increase in Kt/V without risk

explanation: Temperature monitoring/alarms protect against hemolysis and thermal injury.

prompt: Kt/V and URR are measures related to:

options.text: Only blood pressure cuff size
Water softener salt level alone
Dialysis adequacy (urea clearance relative to patient factors)
Fistula thrill strength exclusively

explanation: Adequacy metrics help the care team evaluate whether the prescription is meeting urea clearance goals.

distractorExplanations.d: Thrill assesses access function, not urea kinetic adequacy alone.

prompt: Arterial pressure alarms in the extracorporeal circuit that are excessively negative may suggest:

options.text: Perfect access function always
That UF is turned off
That dialysate is bicarbonate-free by design
Access inflow problems (e.g., stenosis, needle position, clotting) limiting blood flow

explanation: Very negative arterial pressures warrant assessment of needles/access/lines before continuing blindly.

distractorExplanations.a: Alarms indicate a problem to evaluate.
distractorExplanations.c: Bicarbonate dialysate is standard when prescribed.

prompt: Venous pressure rising sharply during treatment may indicate:

options.text: Outflow obstruction such as clotting, kinked line, or infiltrated/poor venous return
That the blood pump is off and everything is fine
Mandatory increase of heparin without assessment
That conductivity is irrelevant forever

explanation: Rising venous pressure is a classic sign to check the venous needle/path for problems.

distractorExplanations.b: Pump-off is not a solution interpretation.
distractorExplanations.c: Anticoagulation changes need RN/protocol—not blind increases.

prompt: A major safety function of the air detector/clamp on the venous line is to:

options.text: Measure residual renal function
Reduce risk of air embolism by alarming and clamping if air is detected
Warm the dialysate only
Calculate target weight automatically from BMI

explanation: Air-in-blood is a life-threatening emergency; detectors are critical safety systems.

prompt: Bicarbonate dialysate is used in part to:

options.text: Increase serum potassium intentionally to high levels
Eliminate the need for infection control
Help correct metabolic acidosis common in kidney failure
Replace heparin in all patients universally

explanation: Buffer base in dialysate supports acid-base correction during HD.

distractorExplanations.a: Hyperkalemia is harmful; dialysate K is prescribed carefully.

prompt: Transmembrane pressure (TMP) relates to:

options.text: Only the patient’s oral temperature
The clinic’s Wi-Fi password
Needle bevel orientation exclusively
Pressure gradient across the dialyzer membrane influencing ultrafiltration

explanation: TMP connects to UF control; unexpected TMP changes need troubleshooting.

distractorExplanations.c: Bevel matters for cannulation, not TMP definition.

prompt: A high-flux dialyzer is characterized by:

options.text: Greater permeability allowing more middle-molecule clearance (and careful UF control)
Zero ultrafiltration capability
No need for water quality standards
Exclusive use without a blood pump

explanation: Flux refers to water/solute permeability characteristics; prescriptions and monitoring still apply.

distractorExplanations.c: Water quality remains essential, especially with high-flux.
distractorExplanations.d: Extracorporeal HD uses a blood pump.

prompt: During dialysis, blood and dialysate typically flow:

options.text: In the same direction only, which maximizes clearance best
In opposite directions (countercurrent) to maximize diffusion gradients
Without any controlled flow rates
Only by gravity without machine control in standard in-center HD

explanation: Countercurrent flow is standard to optimize solute removal efficiency.

distractorExplanations.a: Cocurrent is less efficient for diffusion.

prompt: If dialysate conductivity is out of range and cannot be corrected per protocol, the technician should:

options.text: Bypass all alarms and start anyway
Add table salt to the drain
Not initiate or continue treatment until safe dialysate is verified; escalate per policy
Ignore because conductivity is cosmetic

explanation: Unsafe dialysate composition risks severe electrolyte injury; alarms are not optional.

distractorExplanations.b: Drain additives do not fix dialysate mix.
distractorExplanations.d: Conductivity is a critical safety parameter.

prompt: Pre-dialysis weight is compared with target (dry) weight primarily to determine:

options.text: How much fluid to remove (UF goal)
Needle gauge only
Whether gloves are needed
The patient’s favorite snack

explanation: Interdialytic weight gain drives UF prescription; assess for errors and symptoms.

distractorExplanations.c: PPE is always indicated as required.

prompt: A patient develops sudden chest pain, dyspnea, and a sense of impending doom shortly after starting HD. Suspect and act for:

options.text: Encourage ambulation immediately
Possible air embolism or major cardiopulmonary emergency—stop pump per protocol, clamp lines, emergency response
Increase UF rate aggressively
Give oral fluids quickly as first response

explanation: Air embolism and other emergencies require immediate protocolized response—not routine UF increases.

distractorExplanations.d: Oral fluids are not the air-embolism response.

prompt: Intradialytic hypotension is often related to:

options.text: Always too little UF
Wearing compression sleeves only
Excessive or rapid ultrafiltration relative to vascular refill
Using bicarbonate dialysate as prescribed

explanation: Rapid UF is a common contributor; notify RN, follow protocols (Trendelenburg if used per policy, saline, UF adjust).

distractorExplanations.a: Too little UF causes fluid overload, not typical hypotension from UF.
distractorExplanations.d: Bicarb dialysate is standard therapy, not the usual cause.

prompt: A thrill and bruit in an AV fistula indicate:

options.text: That the access is clotted for sure
That needles are unnecessary forever
Infection is always present
Expected blood flow through a patent access (assess quality/changes)

explanation: Absence or marked change in thrill/bruit can signal thrombosis/stenosis—report promptly.

distractorExplanations.c: Infection has other signs (redness, drainage, fever).

prompt: The “rule of 6s” historically taught for fistula maturation includes concepts such as:

options.text: Adequate depth, diameter, and flow characteristics for successful cannulation (facility protocols vary)
Six heparin vials per treatment mandatory
Six-hour minimum for all treatments regardless of prescription
Six needles in one access simultaneously always

explanation: Maturation criteria guide when an access is ready; follow nephrology/facility standards.

distractorExplanations.d: Standard is typically two needles (or buttonhole variants), not six.

prompt: Steal syndrome related to an AV access may present with:

options.text: Improved grip strength only as a benefit always
Hand pain, coolness, or pallor distal to the access due to ischemia
Isolated hiccups without vascular signs
Elevated dialysate temperature alone

explanation: Ischemic symptoms distal to access need prompt clinical evaluation.

distractorExplanations.d: Dialysate temp is machine/patient safety separate issue.

prompt: If a needle infiltrates during treatment, the technician should generally:

options.text: Increase pump speed to clear the infiltrate
Ignore swelling if the patient says it is fine
Stop the blood pump, discontinue that needle per protocol, apply pressure, and notify the nurse
Flip the arterial and venous lines randomly

explanation: Infiltration risks hematoma and further injury; do not increase flow through a bad needle.

prompt: Muscle cramps during dialysis are commonly associated with:

options.text: Perfect fluid balance always
Wearing two gowns
Documenting vital signs correctly
Volume/electrolyte shifts, especially aggressive UF

explanation: Cramps trigger assessment and RN-directed interventions; prevention includes appropriate UF profiling per order.

distractorExplanations.c: Documentation is required but not the cause.

prompt: First-use syndrome historically associated with some dialyzers may include:

options.text: Chest pain, back pain, or anaphylactoid symptoms related to dialyzer reaction
Improved appetite as the only sign
Mandatory hyperkalemia
Normal uneventful treatment by definition

explanation: Suspect dialyzer reaction with new membrane/sterilant issues; emergency response and reporting apply.

distractorExplanations.b: Reactions are adverse, not appetite benefits.
distractorExplanations.d: It is a complication, not normal.

prompt: Post-dialysis, patients should be assessed for:

options.text: Only whether they liked the TV show
Vital signs, access hemostasis, orthostasis, and ability to safely leave
Skipping blood pressure if they feel well
Immediate heavy weightlifting on the access arm

explanation: Safe discharge/transfer includes hemostasis and hemodynamic stability.

distractorExplanations.a: Comfort is nice but not the clinical endpoint.

prompt: A patient reports numbness and tingling around the mouth and muscle twitching near end of treatment. Consider:

options.text: Normal expected findings requiring no action
Need to increase UF by 2 liters immediately without orders
Possible electrolyte imbalance such as low calcium—notify RN immediately
That conductivity alarms should be silenced permanently

explanation: Neuromuscular symptoms can signal dangerous electrolyte shifts; escalate promptly.

prompt: For a patient with a central venous catheter for dialysis, a key infection-prevention practice is:

options.text: Soaking the catheter in tap water daily
Using the catheter for routine blood draws by any staff without training
Removing dressings with bare unwashed hands always
Aseptic hub care and keeping the exit site clean/dry per protocol

explanation: CVCs carry high bloodstream infection risk; strict asepsis is mandatory.

distractorExplanations.a: Tap water is not aseptic hub care.
distractorExplanations.b: Only trained staff per policy; minimize manipulations.
distractorExplanations.c: Hand hygiene and gloves are required.

prompt: Disequilibrium syndrome is more likely when:

options.text: Urea is lowered too rapidly, especially in first treatments of severe uremia
UF is zero and BUN is already normal
The patient skips one snack
Gloves are powdered

explanation: Neurologic symptoms from osmotic shifts require prevention strategies (shorter/slower initial Rx).

distractorExplanations.b: Risk relates to rapid solute shifts.
distractorExplanations.d: Glove powder is a separate allergy issue.

prompt: Cannulating an aneurysm in a fistula is generally:

options.text: Preferred every treatment for speed
Avoided; cannulate healthy segments per access plan
Required to strengthen the wall
Done without skin prep

explanation: Repeated cannulation of aneurysms risks rupture/bleeding; follow buttonhole or rope-ladder plans as ordered.

distractorExplanations.a: Aneurysms are high risk, not preferred.

prompt: If a patient refuses part of the prescribed treatment, the technician should:

options.text: Secretly increase UF to “make up for it”
Discharge AMA documentation without notifying anyone
Inform the nurse/team, document per policy, and not coerce unsafe deviations alone
Argue until the patient complies regardless of capacity assessment

explanation: Informed refusal is handled by licensed clinicians; technicians escalate and document.

distractorExplanations.d: Capacity/consent issues need proper clinical handling.

prompt: Dialysis water treatment is critical because:

options.text: Patients are exposed to large volumes of water across the dialyzer membrane
Water never contacts the extracorporeal circuit
Municipal water is always sterile and endotoxin-free
Softener salt replaces carbon tanks completely

explanation: Contaminants (chloramine, bacteria, endotoxin, metals) can harm patients if water systems fail.

distractorExplanations.c: Municipal water is not dialysis-grade without treatment.
distractorExplanations.d: Each component has a distinct role.

prompt: Carbon tanks in water treatment primarily help remove:

options.text: All calcium and magnesium exclusively
Chlorine/chloramine
Only dissolved oxygen for taste
Heparin from blood

explanation: Carbon exhaustion risks chloramine breakthrough and hemolysis—monitor per standards.

distractorExplanations.c: DO is not the carbon purpose.
distractorExplanations.d: Heparin is in the blood circuit.

prompt: Water softeners primarily reduce:

options.text: Bacteria to sterile zero always
Endotoxin by definition alone
Calcium and magnesium (hardness)
Patient potassium directly

explanation: Hardness protection helps protect RO membranes; softeners are not sterilizers.

distractorExplanations.d: Patient K is managed via dialysate/diet/meds.

prompt: Reverse osmosis (RO) in dialysis water systems:

options.text: Adds chloramine intentionally
Replaces the need for any monitoring
Is optional decoration plumbing
Removes a broad range of chemical and microbial contaminants when functioning properly

explanation: RO is a cornerstone; reject rates, pressures, and product water quality are monitored.

prompt: Endotoxin in dialysate water is concerning because it can cause:

options.text: Pyrogenic reactions (fever, chills, hypotension)
Improved Kt/V automatically
Faster fistula maturation
Lower venous pressures only as a benefit

explanation: AAMI/facility standards set microbial and endotoxin limits; breaches require action.

prompt: Routine disinfection of the water system and machines is performed to:

options.text: Increase chloramine levels
Control bacterial biofilm and keep microbial counts within standards
Eliminate the need for hand hygiene
Sterilize the patient’s GI tract

explanation: Biofilm is persistent; scheduled disinfection and cultures are part of quality programs.

distractorExplanations.d: Patient GI sterilization is not the goal.

prompt: Hepatitis B precautions in dialysis units emphasize:

options.text: No need for gloves with any blood
Sharing multidose vials freely across patients without asepsis
Vaccination, screening, dedicated equipment/separation practices per CDC recommendations for HBsAg+ patients
Ignoring vaccination status

explanation: Dialysis populations have historically elevated HBV risk; infection control is specialized and strict.

prompt: Hand hygiene should be performed:

options.text: Only at the start of the work week
Never when wearing gloves
Only after lunch
Before and after patient contact and after contact with blood, fluids, or contaminated surfaces

explanation: Gloves do not replace hand hygiene; dialysis is a high blood-exposure environment.

distractorExplanations.b: Hygiene before donning and after doffing gloves.

prompt: A blood spill on a chair should be cleaned:

options.text: Promptly with EPA-registered disinfectant appropriate for bloodborne pathogens per policy, using PPE
With plain water only, no disinfectant
By wiping with a dry paper towel and leaving residue
The next day during monthly cleaning only

explanation: Immediate spill response protects patients and staff.

prompt: Cultures of dialysis water/dialysate that exceed action limits require:

options.text: No documentation
Investigation, disinfection/corrective action, and possible treatment adjustments per medical director/policy
Celebrating improved biofilm
Turning off all carbon tanks permanently

explanation: Quality results drive corrective actions; technicians escalate abnormal findings.

prompt: Cross-contamination risk is reduced when staff:

options.text: Use the same glove pair all day for every patient
Place used needles on the clean supply cart
Avoid carrying contaminated items to clean areas and change gloves/hygiene between dirty and clean tasks
Store lunch in the biohazard bin for cooling

explanation: Clean/dirty workflow separation is foundational in dialysis infection control.

distractorExplanations.d: Food and biohazard do not mix.

prompt: Chloramine breakthrough past carbon tanks is dangerous because it can cause:

options.text: Faster bone healing
Improved appetite only
Automatic heparin neutralization
Hemolysis

explanation: Monitoring residual chlorine/chloramine is a critical daily safety check in many programs.

prompt: Standard precautions mean:

options.text: Treat all blood and certain body fluids as potentially infectious
PPE is never needed with dialysis patients
Only airborne pathogens matter in dialysis
Hand hygiene is optional if busy

explanation: Dialysis care assumes blood exposure risk every treatment.

prompt: Multidose medication vials in dialysis settings should be:

options.text: Entered with a used needle from a patient
Handled with aseptic technique; ideally dedicated per patient when required by policy/outbreak guidance
Stored in the sink
Shared without wiping septa ever

explanation: CDC outbreak lessons emphasize medication preparation safety in dialysis units.

distractorExplanations.a: Never re-enter vials with contaminated needles.

prompt: Ultrafilters on dialysate pathways (where used) help to:

options.text: Increase chloramine concentration
Replace RO membranes permanently without water treatment
Further reduce bacteria/endotoxin in dialysate delivery
Measure patient temperature

explanation: Additional filtration supports ultrapure dialysate goals in some systems.

prompt: Before initiating treatment, machine safety checks typically include verifying:

options.text: Alarms, conductivity/temperature in range, and extracorporeal circuit integrity per checklist
That all alarms are permanently disabled
Skipping prime because the chair is ready
Patient social media passwords

explanation: Pre-treatment checks prevent many adverse events.

prompt: Documentation of dialysis treatment should include:

options.text: Only the patient’s nickname
Vital signs, machine parameters, UF/goal, access, medications given per role, and significant events
No blood pressures if stable
Guessed weights without scale use

explanation: Accurate records support continuity, billing where applicable, and legal/quality review.

prompt: A needle-stick injury should be reported:

options.text: At the annual review only
Never, to avoid paperwork
Immediately per exposure control plan, with first aid and evaluation
Only if the patient requests it

explanation: Timely reporting enables prophylaxis and compliance with OSHA bloodborne pathogen standards.

distractorExplanations.d: Patient request is irrelevant to staff exposure protocol.

prompt: Personal protective equipment for initiating/discontinuing dialysis commonly includes:

options.text: No PPE for any cannulation
Sterile surgical hoods only, never gloves
Sandals without socks as required footwear
Gloves, gown, and face protection as indicated by risk of blood exposure

explanation: Blood sprays/splashes are foreseeable; PPE matches the task.

prompt: If a machine alarm sounds during treatment, the technician should:

options.text: Respond promptly, ensure patient safety, troubleshoot per training, and escalate if unresolved
Mute and ignore for the rest of the shift
Leave the unit without telling anyone
Increase UF to silence pressure alarms always

explanation: Alarms communicate unsafe conditions; systematic response is required.

prompt: QAPI in a dialysis facility focuses on:

options.text: Only decorating the lobby
Ongoing quality assessment and performance improvement for outcomes and safety
Eliminating all patient education
Disabling water quality testing

explanation: Technicians contribute data and participate in improvement activities under the interdisciplinary team.

prompt: Patient confidentiality means technicians should:

options.text: Share interesting cases on TikTok with identifiers
Leave charts open in the waiting room
Discuss patient information only with those involved in care and per privacy rules
Text PHI to friends for advice

explanation: HIPAA and professional ethics apply in dialysis clinics.

distractorExplanations.a: Social media PHI is a breach.

prompt: Electrical and fire safety in the unit includes:

options.text: Blocking exits with supply boxes
Using frayed power cords until they fail
Storing oxygen next to open flames intentionally
Keeping exits clear, knowing extinguisher locations, and not using damaged cords

explanation: Environment of care standards protect patients who cannot easily evacuate.

prompt: When a patient falls in the clinic, the technician should:

options.text: Ensure safety, call for help/RN, avoid moving if injury suspected per protocol, and document/incident report
Help them up quickly without assessment and leave
Hide the event to protect ratings
Ask other patients to lift without staff

explanation: Fall response and reporting are safety and regulatory requirements.

prompt: Scope of practice for dialysis technicians means they:

options.text: Independently prescribe antibiotics
Perform delegated technical tasks per state law/facility policy and escalate clinical decisions to licensed nurses/providers
Change dry weight without any order or RN involvement
Interpret complex ECGs as the sole diagnostician

explanation: Technicians are vital but practice within defined limits.

distractorExplanations.c: Dry weight is a physician/NP order typically.
distractorExplanations.d: Advanced diagnosis is not tech scope.

prompt: A Safety Data Sheet (SDS) is used when:

options.text: Coding ICD-10 for ESRD only
Calculating Kt/V by hand exclusively
Staff need hazard and first-aid information about chemicals used in the facility
Ordering patient meals

explanation: OSHA Hazard Communication requires SDS access for chemicals (acid concentrates, disinfectants, etc.).

distractorExplanations.a: SDS is chemical safety, not coding.

prompt: Machine disinfection documentation is important because it:

options.text: Replaces water cultures forever
Is optional if the machine looks clean
Allows skipping concentrate labeling
Provides evidence that required cleaning/disinfection cycles were completed

explanation: Regulatory surveys review logs; incomplete logs are findings.

prompt: If you are unsure whether a task is within your role, you should:

options.text: Ask the nurse/supervisor and follow policy rather than guessing
Try it once secretly
Ask a patient to teach the invasive step
Post the question with patient identifiers online

explanation: Escalation protects patients and your license/certification pathway.

distractorExplanations.c: Patients are not trainers for invasive skills.

prompt: Emergency oxygen and suction/AED access knowledge is important so that staff can:

options.text: Decorate the crash cart
Respond rapidly to respiratory/cardiac emergencies in the unit
Use oxygen to dust equipment
Store personal bags on the defibrillator

explanation: Dialysis patients have high cardiovascular risk; drills and equipment readiness matter.

prompt: Accurate pre/post weights require:

options.text: Estimating weight by appearance
Using a different random scale without noting it when values conflict without investigation
Same scale conditions when possible, shoes/outerwear consistency, and documented values
Weighing after UF is completed only for pre-weight

explanation: Weight errors directly cause UF errors and patient harm.

distractorExplanations.d: Pre-weight is before treatment fluid removal.
